Home / Reading / Books for Single Dads
Reading

Worth reading.

A short list of books that are actually useful — for parenting, co-parenting, your own head, and building a life that works.

There's no shortage of parenting books. Most of them were written for two-parent households. These are the ones worth your time as a single dad — practical, honest, and not preachy. Titles link to Amazon if you want to grab a copy.

Co-parenting and divorce

  • Mom's House, Dad's House — Isolina Ricci
    The most practical book on building a workable two-home arrangement. Focused on reducing conflict and building systems that work for the kids. Written by a family therapist and mediator who has seen thousands of these situations.
  • Coparenting 101 — Deesha Philyaw & Michael Thomas
    Written by a formerly married couple who co-parent well. Direct, personal, and genuinely useful. Covers the communication and logistics of co-parenting without a lot of theory.
  • Joint Custody with a Jerk — Julie A. Ross & Judy Corcoran
    Specifically for high-conflict co-parenting situations. Strategies for managing a difficult co-parent without escalating things — and without losing your mind.

Parenting

  • How to Talk So Kids Will Listen & Listen So Kids Will Talk — Adele Faber & Elaine Mazlish
    A classic for good reason. Practical communication techniques that work with kids of most ages. Gets you out of the pattern of nagging, threatening, and repeating yourself.
  • The Whole-Brain Child — Daniel J. Siegel & Tina Payne Bryson
    How children's brains actually work and why they behave the way they do. Changes how you interpret meltdowns and gives you tools to respond without escalating.
  • Positive Discipline — Jane Nelsen
    The go-to on discipline that isn't punishment-based. Helps you set real limits without the yelling, bribing, or endless negotiating.
  • The Single Dad's Survival Guide — Michael Klatz
    Written specifically for single fathers. Covers the day-to-day — cooking, household management, childcare, and the emotional piece — from a practical perspective.

Your own head

  • The Dad's Edge — Larry Hagner
    Focused on being a present, engaged father — the mindset piece more than tactics. Hagner is a single dad and runs the Good Dads community, so the perspective is genuine.
  • No More Mr. Nice Guy — Robert Glover
    Widely read among men going through divorce and co-parenting conflict. About breaking patterns of people-pleasing and building a more direct, self-respecting way of operating. Polarizing but worth reading.
  • The Body Keeps the Score — Bessel van der Kolk
    If you've been through a high-conflict divorce or custody battle, this book explains what that kind of sustained stress actually does to your body and brain — and what actually helps. Dense but important.

Finances

  • The Total Money Makeover — Dave Ramsey
    The most accessible personal finance book for someone starting from a difficult position. The advice is conservative but the framework — eliminate debt, build a buffer, then build wealth — is sound and works for single-income households.
  • I Will Teach You to Be Rich — Ramit Sethi
    More modern and less preachy than Ramsey. Good on automating finances so you're not making decisions about money constantly. Practical for people who aren't naturally interested in personal finance.

Most of these are available through your public library — either in print, as e-books through Libby/OverDrive, or as audiobooks through Libby. Worth checking before buying.

As an Amazon Associate, SingleDads.com earns from qualifying purchases. Book links above are affiliate links — buying through them costs you nothing extra and helps keep this site free. Every title here is a genuine recommendation, not a paid placement.
